summary: Cross-chapter audit of cycle 01 found 19 redundancy findings and 5 consistency findings. REDUNDANCY: The strongest pattern is adjacent-chapter re-introduction in Ch3-Ch4, where the Canopy building exterior is re-described from scratch, Priya is re-introduced with the same dark-hair-plus-early-gray and role-statement package, and Nick repeats his 'I was a painter' self-identifying dialogue verbatim. Ch5 re-describes Dani's paint-under-nails one chapter after Ch4's establishment. Nick's X-Acto scar appears six times across the manuscript, including two adjacent-chapter pairs (Ch1-Ch2, Ch15-Ch16). The evaluation floor sensory baseline (fluorescents, HVAC, beige carpet, mouse clicks) is re-inventoried from scratch in Ch12 and Ch14 after full establishment in Ch4. Rosie's bar interior is re-inventoried in Ch9 two chapters after Ch7. Proof-of-baseline accumulation affects the apartment linseed-oil smell (5 chapters), Dani's paint-under-nails (4 chapters), and Priya's teaching background (5+ mentions). Sentence-opener monotony is severe: 'He' opens 14.4% of all sentences (828/5744), with Ch2 at 36.2% and three other chapters above 24%. CONSISTENCY: The bartender at Rosie's bar has a Sharks tattoo in Ch7 and a blue rose tattoo in Ch9. Bao's reading glasses are on a chain in Ch4 but on a lanyard in Ch17. Nick's sabotage notebook changes from burgundy to black in Ch13 and migrates through three different drawer locations (Ch11, Ch13, Ch15). Tommy's self-reported hourly rate shifts from $21 to $20 between Ch7 and Ch9. Timeline and knowledge-state tracking are clean: all five verifiable day-date combinations (Sept 15/Fri, Sept 18/Mon, Nov 7/Tue, Feb 22/Thu, Mar 8/Fri) are correct, and no character acts on premature knowledge.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L515: 'Dark hair was pinned back off her face, early gray showing at the temples... I run the evaluation floor here'; Ch4 L632: 'dark hair showing early gray that she hadn't bothered to dye... I run the image-evaluation team here'
redundancy_findings.problem: Priya Acharya is fully introduced in Ch3 (L515-517) with dark-hair-plus-early-gray physical description and 'I run the evaluation floor' role statement. Ch4 (L632-634), one chapter later, re-introduces her with near-synonymous phrasing: 'dark hair showing early gray' and 'I run the image-evaluation team here.' A reader who just met Priya in Ch3 receives the same identity package again.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch4 L632-634, cut the hair-color-plus-gray descriptor and the 'I run' role statement. Priya can be re-entered through a single new detail (the blazer, the teacher's smile) without re-teaching her appearance or title.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4's first description of Priya contains no reference to her hair color, gray hair, or a self-statement of her role/title. She is identified by name and at most one new physical or behavioral detail.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L493: 'CCA. California College of the Arts. I was a painter.'; Ch4 L674: 'I was a painter. Canvas, oil paint. Landscapes, some illustration work.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Nick delivers the self-identifying line 'I was a painter' in Ch3 (L493) during his interview, then repeats it nearly verbatim in Ch4 (L674) when Tommy asks what he did before. The reader already knows Nick's painter identity from the previous chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch4 L674, replace the full 'I was a painter' declaration with a shorter, deflective version — e.g., a shrug at his paint-stained hands, Tommy already knowing from Priya, or Nick noting internally that he's giving the same answer he gave yesterday.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 does not contain the verbatim phrase 'I was a painter' or any equivalent full self-identification of Nick as a painter. The information may be conveyed obliquely or through Tommy's reaction.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L698: 'Under two of her fingernails, in the cuticle lines, he could see the remnants of pigment. Not polish. Paint. Green and something darker, possibly burnt umber. The kind of stain that didn't come off with soap'; Ch5 L913: 'The way she picked at the paint residue under her nails when she was thinking, a gesture so habitual she probably didn't know she did it'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dani's paint-under-nails is established in Ch4 (L698) as a full introductory passage with color detail and narrator reflection. Ch5 (L913), one chapter later, re-describes the same physical trait with only a slight shift from visual to behavioral framing.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch5 L913, cut or heavily compress the paint-residue detail. The sentence can reference the gesture ('the way she picked at her nails when she was thinking') without re-teaching that paint lives under them.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5's reference to Dani's nail-picking does not re-explain that the residue is paint or describe its properties. The gesture itself may remain.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L369: 'The San Leandro business park was off Davis Street, a collection of two-story office buildings set back from the road behind parking lots. The buildings were beige stucco with tinted windows...three ornamental pear trees'; Ch4 L608: 'The building was the color of spackle...The stucco had probably been white once...the gray-beige of anything left too long under Bay Area weather...trimmed junipers lining the walkway'
redundancy_findings.problem: The Canopy building exterior is described from scratch in Ch3 (L369: stucco, parking lot, landscaping, business park) and again from scratch in Ch4 (L608: stucco color, parking lot, landscaping, business park). Both passages orient the reader to the same exterior as if arriving for the first time. Ch4 should enter through change or Nick's emotional state rather than re-describing stucco and landscaping.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch4 L608, replace the full exterior re-description with a brief acknowledgment of return — e.g., 'The building looked the same as interview day' or enter directly through Nick's nervousness. The lobby directory, elevator, and stairwell are new sub-spaces and can stay; the stucco/parking-lot/landscaping inventory should be cut.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4's opening does not re-describe the building's stucco color, the parking lot, or the landscaping. Nick enters without re-teaching the reader what the exterior looks like.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1485: 'sticky wooden tables, a pool table with a rip in the felt that nobody was going to fix, a jukebox loaded with music from a decade nobody in the bar had lived through consciously'; Ch9 L1751: 'The tables had a film on them that never fully dried. The pool table's felt had a three-inch tear...There was a jukebox in the corner that accepted quarters and sometimes produced music'
redundancy_findings.problem: Rosie's bar interior is fully described in Ch7 (L1485: sticky tables, pool-table rip, jukebox) and re-inventoried from scratch in Ch9 (L1751: tables, pool-table tear, jukebox). The same three fixtures are re-described with different wording but identical orienting function.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch9 L1751, cut the full interior re-inventory. Enter the bar through the current scene's energy — the throughput-week exhaustion, the group dynamic. The bar's name (Rosie's) and the burned-out neon sign are new details that can stay. The tables, pool-table tear, and jukebox should not be re-cataloged.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 does not re-describe the bar's table surface, pool-table felt condition, or jukebox. The bar is entered through the current visit's context.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L624-626: 'institutional fluorescent, the flat shadowless wash...HVAC pushing recycled air through ceiling vents...the staccato click of mice'; Ch12 L2379: 'The hum of HVAC and the flat continuous murmur of keyboards and mouse clicks...The beige carpet absorbed everything. It was a room designed to absorb everything.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The evaluation floor's sensory baseline (fluorescents, HVAC hum, beige carpet, mouse clicks) is established in Ch4 (L624-626). Ch12 (L2379) re-inventories the identical sensory package without entering through change. By Ch12 this floor has been re-described in Ch5, Ch7, and Ch8 as well.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch12 L2379, replace the sensory inventory with what is specific to this scene: Nick's heightened surveillance of coworkers rating his paintings. The floor can be invoked by a single detail rather than the full HVAC-carpet-fluorescent package.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12's entry to the evaluation floor does not re-describe HVAC hum, fluorescent quality, or carpet color. The floor is entered through Nick's emotional state and the specific action of the scene.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L624: 'The light was institutional fluorescent, the flat shadowless wash...beige carpet'; Ch14 L2741: 'The HVAC hummed. The fluorescents threw their flat wash across the beige carpet.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 (L2741) re-inventories the evaluation floor's baseline (HVAC, fluorescents, beige carpet) before pivoting to 'the morning rhythm was absent.' The wrongness is the news; the baseline re-inventory is not. This is the 5th+ time these elements have been cataloged.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch14 L2741, cut the HVAC/fluorescent/carpet sentence and open directly on the wrongness: the absent morning rhythm, the people looking at each other, phones out. The reader knows what the floor normally sounds like.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14's floor entry does not re-list HVAC, fluorescents, or beige carpet before introducing the scene's disruption.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L99: 'His right index finger had a small white scar from an X-Acto knife, from cutting mats for his last gallery show at Vera Lux'; Ch2 L338: 'the small scar on his right index finger from the X-Acto knife he'd slipped with three years ago while cutting a mat for a frame'
redundancy_findings.problem: Nick's X-Acto scar is introduced with its origin story in Ch1 (L99). Ch2 (L338), one chapter later, re-describes the same scar with a near-synonymous origin ('cutting a mat for a frame' vs. 'cutting mats for his last gallery show'). Total manuscript occurrences: 6 (Ch1, Ch2, Ch9, Ch15, Ch16, Ch18).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch2 L338, the scar can be referenced briefly ('the X-Acto scar') without re-narrating its origin. The paragraph's power is in the hands becoming someone else's hands; the scar's backstory was already delivered in Ch1.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2's reference to the scar does not re-explain its origin (X-Acto knife, cutting mats, gallery show). The scar may be named but its backstory is not retold.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L2984: 'The scar from the X-Acto knife was still there, a thin white seam across the pad'; Ch16 L3263: 'the scar on his right index finger flex when he clenched it'
redundancy_findings.problem: Nick's X-Acto scar is described in Ch15 (L2984) and again in Ch16 (L3263), adjacent chapters. By Ch16 this is the 5th of 6 total occurrences (Ch1, Ch2, Ch9, Ch15, Ch16, Ch18).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch16 L3263, replace the scar reference with a different gesture of concealment — e.g., Nick folding his hands, pressing them flat, or gripping the table edge. The reader does not need to be reminded of the scar one chapter after Ch15's detailed mention.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 does not reference the X-Acto scar. Nick's tension during the meeting is conveyed through a different physical gesture.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L99: 'small white scar from an X-Acto knife, from cutting mats for his last gallery show'; Ch18 L3703: 'The small scar on his right index finger from an X-Acto knife he'd slipped with in his twenties'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch18 (L3703) re-describes the X-Acto scar with near-verbatim phrasing from Ch1 ('small scar on his right index finger from an X-Acto knife'). This is the 6th occurrence. The full-origin re-description reads as if re-introducing the scar to a reader who has encountered it five times.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch18 L3703, the hands passage is thematically important and should stay, but the scar can be referenced with minimal text ('the old scar') rather than re-teaching its origin. The calluses carry the paragraph's weight; the scar origin is redundant.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18's hands passage references the scar in three words or fewer, without re-stating its cause (X-Acto knife) or context.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L698: 'Under two of her fingernails, in the cuticle lines, he could see the remnants of pigment'; Ch5 L913: 'paint residue under her nails'; Ch13 L2632: 'Under her nails, in the cuticles, he could see the faint residue of paint, not fresh, not from today, but stubborn enough that soap and water didn't get it all'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dani's paint-under-nails appears for the 3rd time in Ch13 (L2632), with nearly identical framing to Ch4 ('Under her nails, in the cuticles...residue of paint'). The detail that paint residue is stubborn and soap doesn't remove it was already taught in Ch4 L698.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch13 L2632, compress to a brief reference ('the paint that never fully left her hands') without re-explaining location (cuticles) or persistence (soap doesn't remove it). The scene's emotional weight is the kiss and withdrawal.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not re-describe where the paint residue sits or explain its persistence. Any reference to Dani's hands uses at most a passing clause.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L698: first paint-under-nails; Ch5 L913: second; Ch13 L2632: third; Ch17 L3522: 'her left hand, the one not on the mouse, picking at the paint residue under her thumbnail, the residue from the murals she hadn't painted in years, the last physical trace, which she'd never fully scraped away'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dani's paint-under-nails appears for the 4th time in Ch17 (L3522), now with an extended explanatory gloss ('the residue from the murals she hadn't painted in years, the last physical trace'). The reader has been shown this detail three times already.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L3522, keep the gesture (picking at her thumbnail) but cut the explanatory clause about the residue's origin and significance. The sentence's force is in the context — Dani under threat, reverting to a comfort gesture — not in re-explaining the gesture's provenance.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's reference to Dani's nail-picking does not explain that the residue is from her murals or that she hasn't painted in years. The gesture stands without editorial annotation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L676: 'Priya used to teach high school art'; Ch6 L1007: 'She'd mentioned once...that she'd taught high school art for twelve years'; Ch9 L1837-1845: full David Herrera teaching anecdote; Ch17 L3550: 'the team lead who'd left a teaching career after her body made this same sound in a different bathroom'
redundancy_findings.problem: Priya's teaching background is stated or re-stated five-plus times: Ch4 L676, Ch6 L1007, Ch9 L1837 (full anecdote), Ch17 L3550, Ch18 L3647. By Ch17, 'left a teaching career' re-proves a biographical fact the reader has known since Ch4 and heard elaborated in Ch9.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L3550, cut the teaching-career reference. The bathroom scene's power is in the sound itself and the recognition that Priya has broken. Identification can work through simpler means ('the voice he'd heard managing a floor for a year') without re-invoking her pre-Canopy career.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's bathroom passage does not reference Priya's teaching career or prior bathroom breakdowns in schools. Her identity is established through her current role.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch6 L1246: 'faint old smell of linseed oil'; Ch11 L2193: 'The apartment smelled faintly of linseed oil and turpentine...fainter now'; Ch13 L2510-2518: 'The apartment smelled wrong...It used to smell like linseed oil and turpentine...Now it smelled like nothing'; Ch15 L2988: 'The apartment smelled like dust and old turpentine, fading. That smell had once been the dominant note in the room: linseed, solvent, mineral pigment, the smell of labor that left something behind. Now it was residue.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The apartment's fading-paint-smell arc is described in Ch6, Ch11, Ch13, and Ch15. By Ch15 (L2988), this is the 4th time the reader is told that linseed oil and turpentine used to define the apartment and now they're fading. Ch13 already stated 'it smelled like nothing.' Ch15 re-explains the same loss with a longer passage listing the individual components.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch15 L2988, compress the smell paragraph to one sentence or cut it. The apartment's transformation is well established by Ch13. If the smell must appear, enter through contrast ('the turpentine residue was gone now') rather than re-inventorying what it used to be.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch15 does not re-list the components of the apartment's former smell (linseed, solvent, mineral pigment) or re-narrate the arc of their fading. Any smell reference is one sentence or shorter.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 L2510: 'The apartment smelled wrong...Now it smelled like nothing'; Ch15 L2988: 'smelled like dust and old turpentine, fading...Now it was residue'; Ch18 L3743: 'stopped opening the windows months ago, around the time the smell of linseed oil and turpentine had faded from the room and there was nothing left to ventilate'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch18 (L3743) re-states the apartment-smell-fading baseline for the 5th time. The information that linseed oil and turpentine have faded from the room was delivered in Ch13 and Ch15. 'Nothing left to ventilate' restates 'smelled like nothing' (Ch13) and 'it was residue' (Ch15).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch18 L3743, cut the explanation of why the windows are closed. The windows being closed is enough; the reader does not need to be re-told that the paint smell faded. The paragraph's focus should be the painting attempt.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18 does not re-explain the apartment's lost paint smell. The windows may be noted as closed without attribution to the smell arc.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 has 115 'He'-opened sentences out of 318 total sentences (36.2%). Manuscript-wide: 828/5744 (14.4%). The 7% threshold means Ch2 has more than 5x the acceptable density.
redundancy_findings.problem: Chapter 2 has the highest 'He'-opener density in the manuscript at 36.2% — more than one in three sentences begins with 'He.' This creates a monotonous rhythm that flattens the prose. The chapter covers Nick's solitary coding study and Canopy application, which generates long stretches of 'He did X. He did Y. He did Z' narration.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary sentence openings in Ch2 by leading with objects ('The screen...'), temporal markers ('By Christmas...'), subordinate clauses ('When the patterns repeated...'), or sensory details ('The coffee tasted...'). Target bringing Ch2 below 15% He-openers, which requires converting roughly 70 of the current 115 He-opened sentences to other structures.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2's He-opener density is at or below 15% of total sentences. No sequence of five or more consecutive sentences begins with 'He.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 has 101 'He'-opened sentences out of 361 total sentences (28.0%). Manuscript-wide: 828/5744 (14.4%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Chapter 3 has 28.0% He-opener density, nearly 4x the 7% threshold. The interview chapter alternates between Nick's internal state and action, both heavily He-led. The interview dialogue provides some relief, but the surrounding narration compounds the monotony.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Vary sentence openings in Ch3 using the same strategies as R-16. The interview setting offers natural alternatives: 'The room...', 'Kevin's screen...', 'Priya's tablet...', 'On the monitor...'. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3's He-opener density is at or below 15% of total sentences.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 has 73 'He'-opened sentences out of 279 total sentences (26.2%). Manuscript-wide: 828/5744 (14.4%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Chapter 8 has 26.2% He-opener density. The chapter covers Nick working alone on the night floor, studying the calibration system — a solitary activity that collapses into He-led narration. The technical content (QA dashboards, calibration anchors) offers natural non-He openings that are underused.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Lead sentences with system elements ('The dashboard...', 'Calibration anchors...', 'Each gold lozenge...'), temporal markers ('By ten PM...'), or perceptual details ('The fluorescent hum...'). Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8's He-opener density is at or below 15% of total sentences.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13 has 67 'He'-opened sentences out of 279 total sentences (24.0%). Manuscript-wide: 828/5744 (14.4%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Chapter 13 has 24.0% He-opener density. The chapter covers Nick's failed painting attempt, Luna's recital, and the kiss with Dani — scenes rich with sensory and interpersonal detail that could anchor sentence openings but instead default to 'He.'
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Lead sentences with scene elements ('The canvas...', 'Luna's voice...', 'Dani's sweater...'), spatial details ('On the easel...', 'In the gym...'), or Dani/Luna as sentence subjects where the scene's focus warrants it. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13's He-opener density is at or below 15% of total sentences.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1507: 'The bartender was a heavyset woman with a Sharks tattoo on her forearm who poured without comment or judgment'; Ch9 L1851: 'The bartender was a woman in her fifties with a faded blue rose tattoo on her forearm and arms that suggested she'd done work heavier than this before she'd done this'
consistency_findings.problem: The bartender at Rosie's bar is described with a Sharks tattoo on her forearm in Ch7 (L1507) and a faded blue rose tattoo on her forearm in Ch9 (L1851). Both scenes are at the same bar; both bartenders pour without comment; nothing signals a different person. The physical description also shifts from 'heavyset woman' to 'woman in her fifties.'
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch9 L1851, change the tattoo to match Ch7's Sharks tattoo, or change Ch7 to match Ch9. Pick one description and use it consistently. If they are meant to be different bartenders, add a signal ('a different woman behind the bar tonight').
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The bartender's tattoo description is identical in Ch7 and Ch9, or the text explicitly establishes that a different bartender is working.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch4 L640: 'He wore reading glasses on a thin chain'; Ch17 L3392: 'settling his reading glasses into the case on the lanyard'; Ch17 L3420: 'He smoothed his shirt, adjusted the lanyard'
consistency_findings.problem: Bao's reading glasses are attached to 'a thin chain' in Ch4 (L640) but to 'the lanyard' in Ch17 (L3392, L3420). A glasses chain and a lanyard are different objects; 'lanyard' is used elsewhere in the novel exclusively for badge/ID holders (Ch3 L375, Ch4 L622, Ch10 L1994). The continuity sheet specifies 'reading glasses on a chain.'
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L3392, change 'the lanyard' to 'the chain' or 'the thin chain.' In L3420, 'adjusted the lanyard' should refer to his badge lanyard (which he has separately) or be changed to 'adjusted the chain.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's references to Bao's reading glasses use 'chain' (matching Ch4) and are not conflated with his badge lanyard.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2251: 'a Stillman & Birn Alpha series...with deep burgundy covers that had cost him fourteen dollars at Blick'; Ch13 L2694: 'It had a black cover and a bent corner and sat tucked in the drawer beside his bed'; Ch15 L2984: 'he took the burgundy Stillman & Birn sketchbook out of the drawer beside the couch'
consistency_findings.problem: Nick's sabotage notebook is established as having 'deep burgundy covers' in Ch11 (L2251) and confirmed as 'burgundy' in Ch15 (L2984), but Ch13 (L2694) describes it as having 'a black cover.' The notebook is the same object (repurposed for corruption logs) and should not change color.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch13 L2694, change 'a black cover' to 'a burgundy cover' or 'deep burgundy covers' to match Ch11's establishment.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13's description of the sabotage notebook uses 'burgundy' (not 'black') for its cover color.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2271-2273: notebook stored in 'the drawer' under the kitchen counter, 'vanished under the menus without showing a corner'; Ch13 L2694: 'sat tucked in the drawer beside his bed'; Ch15 L2984: 'out of the drawer beside the couch'
consistency_findings.problem: Nick's sabotage notebook migrates through three different storage locations without any narrated move: under the kitchen counter (Ch11 L2271), beside his bed (Ch13 L2694), and beside the couch (Ch15 L2984). The continuity sheet says 'Hidden in a kitchen drawer beneath takeout menus.'
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Pick one consistent storage location (the kitchen drawer per the continuity sheet) and use it in Ch13 and Ch15. If the notebook moves, narrate the move.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The sabotage notebook's storage location is consistent across Ch11, Ch13, and Ch15, or any change in location is explicitly narrated.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1543: Tommy says 'I'm making twenty-one bucks an hour'; Ch9 L1847: Tommy says 'Rating images for twenty bucks an hour'
consistency_findings.problem: Tommy's self-reported hourly rate is $21 in Ch7 (L1543) and $20 in Ch9 (L1847). The continuity sheet specifies 'about $21/hour.' While colloquial rounding is possible, the $1 discrepancy in self-reported pay within two chapters reads as a tracking error.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch9 L1847, change 'twenty bucks' to 'twenty-one bucks' to match Ch7's established figure and the continuity sheet.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Tommy's self-reported hourly rate is '$21' (or 'twenty-one') in both Ch7 and Ch9.
